data.title: Wall Street rally loses more steam as stocks hit fresh session lows
data.description:
Wall Street's furious rebound on Tuesday came under some pressure after a U.S. government official doused hopes of any tariff exemptions and a Federal Reserve speaker largely spoke negatively about the trade barriers.   

The three major averages hit fresh session lows. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.79% at 5,102.03 points in midday trade. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.57% at 15,692.84 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +1.22% at 38,427.05 points.

The gauges opened solidly in negative territory and at one point were up nearly +4% each.

Over in the fixed-income markets, a selloff in bonds extended from Monday and pushed U.S. Treasury yields higher. According to Apollo Asset Management, the selling may be linked to the unwinding of the "basis trade." The 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 9 basis points to 4.25%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 11 basis points to 3.83%.  

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page. 

U.S. Trade Representative ambassador Jamieson Greer said that President Trump had been clear that he won't issue any tariff exemptions any time soon. Greer's comments came in a U.S. Senate hearing. 

Chicago Fed president Austan Goolsbee on public radio said Trump's tariffs were way bigger than anticipated, adding that the central bank couldn't just rely on hard data to gauge the impact of the tariffs.   

Earlier, hopes of a trade deal had helped shore up sentiment following a historic few days of market volatility. Trump announced his sweeping reciprocal tariffs - the steepest American trade barriers in over a century - last Wednesday. What followed was carnage as markets across the world plummeted on fears of a global trade war destroying economic growth.

The S&P 500 (SP500) cratered more than -10% over last Thursday and Friday, one of its biggest two-day declines in four decades. Investors then returned from the weekend and proceeded to whipsaw through a topsy-turvy Monday. The S&P swung from one extreme to another, gaining as much as +3.40% and also losing as much as -4.71%. The benchmark index briefly swung into bear market territory.

"The stock market briefly touched bear market territory on Monday, and for right now, we're still within the bounds of a correction and not a deeper bear market, even though this past week has been extremely uncomfortable," Clark Bellin, president and chief investment officer at Bellwether Wealth, said.  

"While there may be more downside ahead, we remind investors that there have been many examples of corrections that see a near 20% peak to trough decline, such as in 2018 and in 1998, only to see a steep rally soon after that is sustained for some time," he said. 

"Regardless of whether or not we see a recession, the stock market will at some point price-in the recovery. The stock market moves ahead of economic activity, and this is an important reminder for those who fear continued stock market downside if we see a recession materialize," Bellin added. 

Market participants this week will have plenty to look forward to, with upcoming economic data on inflation, several Federal Reserve speakers, and the start of the first quarter earnings season.

data.title: Retailer's next big test as inflation fatigue meets tariff turmoil
data.description:
Faced with the possibility of a protracted trade war, retailers across the U.S. are looking at their long-term business plans and ways to offset higher tariffs with operational cuts and price hikes.

Corporate giants like Nike (NYSE:NKE), Walmart (NYSE:WMT), and Target (NYSE:TGT) can absorb the increased costs for a time without passing them onto the consumer in the hopes that the tariffs will be lifted and the temporary hit to their bottom line won’t spook investors.

But for small and medium-sized retailers, the challenge is more acute as higher prices could drive consumers to cheaper options, while higher costs will be much harder to absorb for a company with already razor-thin margins.

In an interview with CNBC, Matt Priest, CEO of Footwear Distributors and Retailers of America laid out the hard choices both manufacturers, and consumers face in this new tariff-driven environment.

“Eighty-seven percent of retail executives see the headwinds right in front of us, and we expect that number to reach 100%,” Priest said, ruling out on-shoring production as a viable option given that tariffs also apply to inputs as well as the machinery used in textile manufacturing.

“We see a consumer that is nervous,” Priest added. “Leading up to the inauguration, footwear sales were up 14%. That number has dropped 11 points in the following 10 weeks.

“We’re asking consumers to play the long game, but from an inflation perspective, consumers have been playing the long game for years…they’re worn out,” Priest admitted.

And according to data from the National Retail Federation, while spending year-over-year continued to grow as the economy remained strong, it was down in February as tariff concerns weighed on consumers.

“Unease about the probability of inflation and paying higher prices for non-discretionary goods has the value-conscious consumer spending less and saving more,” said NRF president Matthew Shay.  

For specific retailers, the headwinds vary based on size and supply chain complexities. According to a study by Goldman Sachs, the degree to which a company is insulated from tariff exposure also conveys how likely they will be to pass on the cost to consumers.

Goldman’s team led by Brooke Roach shows Yeti (NYSE:YETI), Shark Ninja (NYSE:SN), Torrid Holdings (NYSE:CURV) and Nike (NYSE:NKE) as the most exposed to import tariffs while at the same time with a high U.S sales exposure, while Canada Goose (NYSE:GOOS), Burlington (NYSE:BURL), Nordstrom (NYSE:JWN), and Macy’s (NYSE:M) as the least exposed. Of all of these, Torrid Holdings (NYSE:CURV), with 49% of their merchandise sourced from China, is the least able to pass along higher prices.

data.title: Applied Materials CEO acquires shares worth ~$6.87M
data.description:
• Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) said in an SEC filing yesterday that President and CEO Gary Dickerson bought shares worth ~$6.87M.
  • A total of 48,581 shares of common stock were acquired for a weighted average price per share of $137.29, and 1,419 shares for $137.7.

data.title: SA Analyst Rating Downgrades: MSFT, TSLA, NVDA, ON, IIPR, PLAY, YETI
data.description:
Here are the latest Seeking Alpha analyst rating downgrades:

Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT). Buy to hold by Income Generator. "Microsoft’s recent earnings beat expectations, but revenue growth and net income rates have slowed, leading to a downgrade to a 'hold' rating."

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). Hold to sell by Tech Stock Pros. "We think Tesla's unlikely to hit its 2025 growth goals outlined by Musk last year, and we think the whole turnaround effort around cheaper cars will be much more difficult with tariffs and come at the expense of margins, which have been under pressure since the price wars began and sat at 16.3% in 4Q24. We think investors are not gonna see the sudden comeback moment in the near term, at least not one based on fundamentals." 

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). Buy to hold by Zen Analyst. "The 2025 Liberation Day tariffs impose significant duties on tech imports, impacting Nvidia's costs, supply chains, and market access, particularly in Asia…Nvidia's ability to adapt through supply chain adjustments and strategic product releases will be crucial in navigating these tariffs and maintaining its market position."

ON Semiconductor. (NASDAQ:ON). Buy to sell by The Techie. "I think Onsemi has much more downside ahead, and I foresee a steep decline before a rebound, so I would advise investors to step out now and only step back in when there’s a clear vision of an EV recovery or more visibility on the tariff situation." 

Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR). Hold to sell by The Dividend Collectuh. 

Dave & Buster's (NASDAQ:PLAY). Hold to sell by Michael Wiggins De Oliveira.

Yeti (NYSE:YETI). Buy to hold by OA Research.

data.title: Taiwan Semi could see potential $1B or more in fine in US probe - report
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) could face a fine of $1B or more to resolve a U.S. export control probe over a chip it made that landed in a Huawei processor, Reuters reported citing, people with knowledge of the matter.

The U.S. Department of Commerce has been probing TSM's work for the Chinese company Sophgo. The design company's TSM-made chip had ended up in Chinese tech giant Huawei's high-end Ascend 910B AI processor, the report added.

Last year in December, the Biden administration moved to blacklist Sophgo, after it got access to TSM's chip. In October last year, TSM had halted shipments to Sophgo after its chip ended up in the Ascend 910B processor by Huawei, which faces import restrictions from the U.S. on several types of items. Sophgo had then said that the investigation by the U.S. Commerce Department is not related to Sophgo and that it has never been engaged in a business relationship with Huawei.

The potential $1B or more fine comes from export control regulations allowing for a fine of up to two times the value of transactions that violate the rules, the report noted.

TSM, the U.S. Department of Commerce and Sophgo did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

TSM spokesperson Nina Kao told the news agency that the company is committed to complying with the law and added that TSM has not supplied to Huawei since mid-September 2020 and that they are cooperating with the Commerce Department.

TSM had said in October 2024, to Seeking Alpha that it "proactively communicated with the US Commerce Department regarding the matter in the report."  and that the company was not aware of TSM being the subject of any investigation at that time. TSM had alerted U.S. officials after TechInsights took apart a Huawei product and found one of TSM's chips, according to a prior report.

Huawei's Ascend 910B chips are used in China to train large language models, or LLMs, that power AI-chatbots, and were made to rival chips from Nvidia (NVDA).

TSM made nearly three million chips in recent years which matched the design ordered by Sophgo and likely ended up with Huawei, the report added citing Lennart Heim, a researcher at RAND's Technology and Security and Policy Center in Arlington, Virginia, who is tracking Chinese developments in AI.

data.title: Fannie Mae fires over 100 employees for misconduct
data.description:
The Federal Housing Finance Agency announced on Tuesday that more than 100 Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) employees were fired for "engaging in unethical conduct, including facilitating fraud."

The statement did not provide any details on the type of unethical conduct or fraud that it discovered. 

"In President Trump’s housing market, there is no room for fraud, mortgage fraud, or any other deceitful act that can jeopardize the safety and soundness of the housing industry," said William J. Pulte, chairman of the Board of Directors of Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA). Pulte is also director of the FHFA, which oversees both FNMA and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC).

Priscilla Almodovar, president and CEO of Fannie Mae, said, "We hold our employees to the highest standards, and we will continue to do so."

Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) stock rose 3.8% in Tuesday midday trading.

data.title: Marjorie Taylor Greene discloses purchases of Apple, Amazon, others
data.description:
At least one member of Congress bought the dip.

Amid the recent tariff-induced market sell-off, Congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene disclosed that she purchased positioned in companies across several sectors, including tech stocks.

A new congressional trading form filed by Greene disclosed that she purchased positioned in companies on April 3 and April 4, including Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B), and Dell Technologies (DELL), worth between $1,001 and $15,000 each. 

The 50-year-old Congresswoman representing the 14th district in Georgia also purchased stakes in Caterpillar (CAT), FedEx (FDX), Impinj (PI), J.P. Morgan (JPM), Lam Research (LRCX), Lululemon (LULU), Nike (NKE), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), Qualcomm (QCOM), RH (RH), Southern Company (SO) and UPS (UPS). The value of these stakes were also between $1,001 and $15,000 and occurred during the market sell-off of April 3 and April 4.

Congresswoman Greene has become one of the more active legislators in terms of purchasing stocks. She disclosed several purchases last month during a market sell-off and made purchases in December 2024 and January 2025, a week after President Trump's inauguration.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Microsoft, Google AI deals in crosshairs of Sens. Warren, Wyden
data.description:
• The respective cloud computing deals that Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) have signed with artificial intelligence companies have come under fire from two Democratic lawmakers, who are concerned they could impact competition.
  • “We are concerned that corporate partnerships within the AI sector discourage competition, circumvent our antitrust laws, and result in fewer choices and higher prices for businesses and consumers using AI tools,” Senators Elizabeth Warren and Ron Wyden wrote in a pair of letters. 
  • The senators highlighted the deals that Microsoft and Google have respectively signed with OpenAI and Anthropic. 
  • “Partnerships between [cloud service providers] and AI developers, if left unchecked, may accelerate consolidation of the AI sector, ultimately driving up prices and choking off innovation,” wrote the senators.
  • The letters also seek to understand how much the AI companies have paid the cloud service providers, whether they have exclusive rights to license the AI models and if there are plans for the publicly traded giants to acquire their AI partners.
  • Microsoft has made a multi-billion dollar financial investment into OpenAI, while Google recently invested another $1B into Anthropic.
  • Microsoft and Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

(This story has been updated to include a link to the letter.)

data.title: SA Analyst Rating Upgrades: TSLA, PLTR, DD, LEVI, WBA, UNFI
data.description:
Here are the latest Seeking Alpha analyst rating upgrades:

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). Sell to hold by Stone Fox Capital. "The key investor takeaway is that TSLA stock provides crucial support around $220. If this holds, an investor can play the robotaxi opportunity, with Tesla likely to rally heading into this supposed launch in Austin in a few months. If the stock doesn't hold, an investor probably wants to head towards the sidelines and avoid the stock."

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). Hold to buy by Livy Investment. "There are multiple imminent upside catalysts in place for TSLA stock that shouldn't be overshadowed by ongoing market fears and tariff uncertainties."

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). Sell to hold by Juxtaposed Ideas. "Despite the correction, PLTR remains inflated compared to peers, with the limited near-term recovery catalysts yet deserving a Buy rating."

DuPont (NYSE:DD). Sell to hold by Blue Chip Portfolios. "While the China probe is a clear negative for the stock, I believe the stock's valuation fully reflects this risk. I am upgrading the stock to Hold as I believe the risk-reward is now more balanced than was previously the case due to DuPont's more attractive valuation."

Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI). Hold to buy by Daniel Jones. "Revenue, profits, and most cash flow figures are growing nicely. Shares have also become cheaper and are now attractively priced, especially relative to similar companies."

Walgreens (NASDAQ:WBA). Sell to hold by Envision Research. 

United Natural Foods (NYSE:UNFI). Hold to buy by Caffital Research.

data.title: Interactive Brokers adds Chainlink, Avalanche, Sui to crypto trading offering
data.description:
Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) added more cryptocurrencies to its trading platform, including Chainlink (LINK-USD), Avalanche (AVAX-USD) and Sui (SUI-USD), it said on Tuesday.

The new tokens are available to clients with an account at Zero Hash LLC, a FinCen-registered money service business and a regulated money transmitter that can operate in 51 U.S. jurisdictions

IBKR already offers trading for bitcoin (BTC-USD), Ethereum (ETH-USD), Litecoin (LTC), Bitcoin Cash (BCH-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), Cardano (ADA-USD), Ripple (XRP-USD) and Dogecoin (DOGE-USD). Customers can establish spot crypto positions and hedge their exposure with crypto futures, options and exchange-traded products.

“Recognizing the growing popularity of cryptocurrency trading, we continue to expand the number of digital assets available on our platform,” Steve Sanders, executive vice president of Marketing and Product Development at Interactive Brokers, said in a statement.

The three new tokens are available to clients with an account at Zero Hash LLC, a FinCen-registered money service business and a regulated money transmitter that can operate in 51 U.S. jurisdictions.

Crypto trading commissions at Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) are among the lowest in the space, it said, ranging from 0.12% to 0.18% of trade value based on monthly volume.
